1: The heading of the Temple Sermon establishes it as a direct divine communication: asher hayah el-Yirmiyahu me-et YHWH. This prophetic authority pattern is the same structure Jesus claims: 'You have heard it was said ... but I say to you' (Matthew 5:21-48) — the direct divine voice that supersedes scribal transmission. Jesus's temple actions in the final week are the enacted fulfillment of this sermon.
2: Standing at the gate of the LORD's house to address those entering: Jesus's temple teaching also takes place in the temple courts (Matt 21-25; Mark 12; Luke 20-21). The audience is those entering to worship — those who assume that presence at the temple means covenant standing. Jesus also addresses the assumption that cultic proximity guarantees covenant security.
3: 'Reform your ways and your actions and I will let you live in this place' — the conditional covenant of habitation. Jesus fulfills the unconditioned side of this covenant through his perfect obedience: he is the one who dwells with the Father without condition (John 1:1-2, 18), and through him the conditional requirement is met for those who are united with him (2 Corinthians 5:21). His death at Passover in Jerusalem is the ultimate reformed act that satisfies what Jeremiah's audience refused.
4: 'Do not trust in these deceptive words: the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD.' The triple chant is religious formula emptied of reality — the building invoked but the covenant ignored. Jesus will identify himself as the true temple (John 2:21) and declare that this building will be destroyed (Matthew 24:2). The deceptive confidence in the structure rather than in YHWH is exactly what Jesus dismantles.
5: The conditions: truly reform, administer justice, do not exploit the foreigner, fatherless, widow. The legal-social content of genuine worship. Jesus in Matthew 23:23 condemns those who tithe but 'neglect the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, and faithfulness.' Both Jeremiah's Temple Sermon and Jesus's temple teaching identify the same failure: ritual compliance without covenantal justice.
6: Specific prohibitions: exploitation of the vulnerable, shedding innocent blood, following other gods. The shedding of innocent blood (dam naqi) points toward the ultimate innocent — Jesus, in whose death the long history of shedding innocent blood reaches its climax and its redemptive turning point. The disciples will later recall this context: 'I have sinned by betraying innocent blood' (Matthew 27:4 — Judas using the exact phrase).
7: The land-promise: obedience leads to dwelling in the land given to the ancestors. Jesus reframes the land-promise eschatologically: 'the meek will inherit the earth' (Matthew 5:5 = Psalms 37:11); 'in my Father's house are many rooms ... I go to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2). The promised dwelling that Judah forfeits through disobedience is restored through Christ's obedience — but expanded: not a geographic territory but the new creation.
8: 'You are trusting in deceptive words that can do you no good.' The davar sheqer (deceptive/lying word) — the false security of temple-formula. Jesus warns against the same false security: 'not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 7:21); 'you are like whitewashed tombs' (Matthew 23:27). Both Jeremiah and Jesus unmask the gap between religious performance and covenant reality.
9: The indictment catalog: steal, murder, commit adultery, swear falsely, sacrifice to Baal, follow other gods — and then come stand before YHWH in the temple. The Ten Commandments inverted: the worshippers break the covenant decalogue and then seek covenant blessing. Jesus's Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) internalizes the same commandments: 'you have heard it said you shall not murder, but I say to you everyone who is angry...' — reaching beneath the external act to the heart.
10: 'Come and stand before me in this house that bears my name and say: we are safe.' The performative 'we are safe' (nittzalnu) — ritual declaration of covenant standing. Jesus is the one in whose name all future prayer and covenant access is made (John 14:13-14; Jeremiah 16:23-24). His name replaces the temple as the locus of covenant presence.
11: 'Has this house, which bears my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes?' — Jesus quotes this verse exactly (Mark 11:17: spēlaion lēstōn = den of robbers) when he drives out the temple traders. This is the most explicit NT citation of the Temple Sermon. Jesus applies Jeremiah's judgment speech to his own contemporaries, claiming prophetic continuity: what Jeremiah charged Jerusalem with in 609 BCE, Jesus charges with again in 30 CE. The temple has become what Jeremiah warned it would become.
12: 'Go to Shiloh ... see what I did to it.' The Shiloh precedent: YHWH destroyed the first sanctuary when it was corrupted. Jesus's Olivet Discourse (Matthew 24:1-2) gives the Jerusalem-temple equivalent: 'not one stone here will be left on another.' The destruction of 70 CE is the fulfillment of the Shiloh-pattern warning renewed by Jesus, who renewed Jeremiah's renewal of the same warning.
13: 'I spoke to you again and again, but you did not listen' — the persistent speaking (hashkem vedabber). Jesus identifies himself as the one in whom this persistent divine speech reaches its climax: 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Hebrews 1:2). The Son is the final, definitive word after the long sequence of servants.
14: The destruction of the temple because of its misuse. Fulfilled in 586 BCE; renewed as warning by Jesus (Matthew 24:2); fulfilled again in 70 CE. The theological logic: the temple exists to mediate YHWH's presence; when it becomes an obstacle to that presence (substituting ritual for relationship), it is destroyed. Jesus's body becomes the new temple (John 2:21) — the dwelling of divine presence that cannot be permanently destroyed (John 2:19: 'I will raise it in three days').
15: The expulsion from divine presence — just as YHWH drove out the offspring of Ephraim. The exile from the land is the covenant curse of the Most Terrible: exclusion from YHWH's presence (Leviticus 26:33; Deuteronomy 28:36-37). Jesus's atonement is the reversal of this expulsion: 'I go to prepare a place for you ... that where I am you may also be' (John 14:2-3). His crucifixion — the moment of maximum separation from the Father ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me') — is the bearing of this expulsion so that believers are never expelled.
16: 'Do not pray for this people.' The prophetic intercession ban — the point of no return where judgment becomes fixed. Jesus, by contrast, prays for his people even at the point of maximum sin: 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing' (Luke 23:34). He is the intercessor who continues to plead beyond the point where human intercession fails (Hebrews 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them').
17: YHWH directs Jeremiah to observe what the people are doing in the towns and streets. The omniscient divine attention to human behavior: 'Does he not see my ways and count my every step?' (Job 31:4). Jesus's omniscient observation is consistent: he 'knew all people and needed no one to testify about man, for he himself knew what was in man' (John 2:24-25).
18: The queen of heaven cult — the whole family participating in idolatry together: children gathering wood, fathers lighting the fire, women kneading dough. The comprehensive family participation in false worship is the opposite of the Shema's requirement to teach the commands to your children (Deuteronomy 6:6-9). Jesus comes to 'bring not peace but a sword' (Matthew 10:34-36) — family division over allegiance to him — because idolatrous family systems must be disrupted for covenant faithfulness.
19: 'Is it really me they are provoking? ... Is it not themselves they are harming?' The logic of self-destructive sin: idolatry destroys the idolater, not YHWH. Jesus's teaching on sin follows the same logic: 'what comes out of a person is what defiles him' (Mark 7:20-23). Sin is self-contaminating, not God-insulting in any sense that threatens God; the harm is to the sinner.
20: The wrath poured out on the land — on human, animal, and tree alike. Creation suffers under human sin: Romans 8:19-22 (the creation groaning, subjected to futility because of human failure). Jesus's death and resurrection initiates the re-creation that undoes this comprehensive curse: 'the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay' (Romans 8:21).
21: YHWH's satirical invitation to add burnt offerings to sacrifices and eat the meat: when the covenant relationship is broken, sacrifices become meaningless dietary exercises. Jesus fulfills and supersedes the entire sacrificial system: 'I desire mercy and not sacrifice' (Matthew 9:13; 12:7 quoting Hosea 6:6), and his single offering perfects what the multiplied offerings could not (Hebrews 10:1-14).
22: 'On the day I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, I did not command them about burnt offerings and sacrifices.' The Exodus-covenant priority: relationship and obedience precede sacrifice. Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes Psalms 40:6-8 to make the same point about Jesus: 'sacrifice and offering you did not desire ... then I said, here I am, I have come to do your will.' Jesus fulfills the original intention of the Sinai covenant by offering the obedience that the whole sacrificial system was pointing toward.
23: 'Obey me and I will be your God and you will be my people' — the covenant formula in its simplest form. Jesus is the obedient one who perfectly fulfills the 'obey me' side: 'not my will but yours be done' (Luke 22:42); 'I always do the things that please him' (John 8:29). His perfect obedience enacts the covenant formula on behalf of all who are in him; they become YHWH's people through his covenant-keeping.
24: The stubborn, evil hearts following their own plans — shreruyt libbam hara (the stubbornness of their evil hearts). Jesus diagnoses the same: 'from within, from the heart of man, come evil thoughts' (Mark 7:21). His new covenant promise (Jeremiah 31:33-34) is the answer: 'I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts.' The new heart is the solution to the stubborn heart, accomplished through the Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2; Romans 8:3-4).
25: 'From the day your ancestors came out of Egypt until this very day, I have persistently sent all my servants the prophets.' The servant-prophet pattern. The Parable of the Tenants (Matthew 21:33-46) uses this exact sequence: servants sent to the vineyard → rejected → finally the son → killed. Jesus identifies himself as both the final prophet and the Son who is the climax of the whole sequence. Stephen's speech in Acts 7:51-52 makes the same identification: 'which of the prophets did your ancestors not persecute?... and now they have betrayed and murdered the Righteous One.'
26: Each generation worse than the previous in not listening — the progressive hardening of Israel. Jesus observes the same: 'the sins of the fathers' accumulating, the 'blood of all the prophets shed since the foundation of the world' coming upon 'this generation' (Luke 11:50-51; Matthew 23:35-36). The cross is where the accumulated rejection reaches its apex — and where it is absorbed rather than returned.
27: 'You will speak all these words but they will not listen; you will call but they will not respond.' The prophetic hardening oracle — given in advance so the prophet is not surprised by rejection. Jesus prepares his disciples for the same reception: 'if they persecuted me they will persecute you' (John 15:20); 'a servant is not greater than his master' (John 13:16). Rejection of the prophetic word is rejection of the sender.
28: 'This is the nation that did not obey the LORD their God and refused correction; faithfulness has perished, it has vanished from their lips.' The funeral announcement of faithfulness. Jesus comes as the faithful witness (ho martus ho pistos, Revelation 1:5; Jeremiah 3:14) — the one in whom faithfulness has not perished. He is the faithful Israelite where Israel was unfaithful, making him the representative who can absorb the judgment and restore the relationship.
29: The shaving and lamenting on the hilltops — mourning rites for the LORD's rejection and abandonment of his generation. Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (Matthew 23:37-39; Luke 19:41-44) takes up this same mourning: he weeps over the city whose generation has become the rejected generation of Jeremiah's vision. 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets ... your house is left to you desolate.'
30: Detestable idols in the house bearing YHWH's name — the temple defiled. Jesus enters the temple and drives out those who have made it a marketplace: 'my house shall be called a house of prayer for all nations' (Mark 11:17). He reclaims the holiness of YHWH's name-space. His own body is the holy temple (John 2:21) that replaces the defiled one.
31: Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom — child sacrifice to Molech, called 'the thing I did not command and that never entered my mind.' This valley (Gei Hinnom in Hebrew, Gehenna in Greek) becomes the NT's primary image of final judgment. Jesus uses Gehenna twelve times in the Gospels (Matthew 5:22, 29-30; Jeremiah 10:28; Jeremiah 18:9; Jeremiah 23:15, 33; Mark 9:43-47; Luke 12:5) as the place of judgment — the place where fire consumed the defilements of the city. The valley's history of child sacrifice and later of burning the city's refuse gives Jesus's imagery its force.
32: The renaming: Topheth and the Valley of Ben Hinnom become the Valley of Slaughter because of the mass burial needed. The irony: the place where Israel sacrificed children to Molech becomes the mass grave of the Israeli dead. The NT's Gehenna incorporates this irony: the judgment image derives from the very place of Israel's worst sin. Jesus warns that the final judgment is more terrible than any earthly slaughter — the fire that never goes out (Mark 9:43-48).
33: Corpses as food for birds and beasts, with no one to frighten them away — the maximum desecration, the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:26. Jesus takes this curse in his own death: crucified outside the city, exposed (Colossians 2:15; Galatians 3:13: 'cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree'). But his body is given an honorable burial (John 19:38-42) and raised — the reversal of the curse that those he represents deserved.
34: 'I will silence the voices of the bridegroom and bride.' The judgment-silencing of covenant joy. Jesus identifies himself as the bridegroom (John 3:29; Matthew 9:15; Jeremiah 25:1-13). His presence marks the return of wedding joy: 'while the bridegroom is with them, they cannot fast' (Mark 2:19). The silence of Jeremiah 7:34 is what his arrival reverses; Revelation 19:7 announces the final restoration of this joy: 'Let us rejoice and be glad and give him glory, for the wedding of the Lamb has come.'
